SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper’s main claims are generally supported: it describes a plausible adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling to train joint word/phrase/concept embeddings and presents empirical comparisons against multiple prior methods across several biomedical and general-domain datasets using standard metrics (Spearman’s ρ, cosine, MAP). The mathematical approach appears standard and internally consistent (no derivations/proofs noted as needed), and the experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines and datasets; however, important reproducibility details are omitted in the summary (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and there is no mention of statistical significance testing. Given these minor but relevant omissions and the fact that the method is outperformed on some smaller biomedical datasets, the technical claims are largely but not perfectly supported. | B(4): The paper's main claims are generally supported by empirical evidence: experiments across biomedical and general-domain datasets with multiple strong baselines show the proposed weakly-supervised joint embeddings perform competitively, and reported analyses link embeddings to ontology structure. The mathematical approach (adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling and concept-normalized updates) is plausible and no formal derivations appear necessary or incorrect. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and no statistical testing is reported; additionally the method is outperformed on some smaller biomedical datasets and phrase-approximation beats direct concept embeddings on real-entity data, which the authors acknowledge. These issues are minor relative to the core technical contribution but prevent a full, flawless endorsement.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper’s main claims are generally supported: it describes a plausible adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling to train joint word/phrase/concept embeddings and presents empirical comparisons against multiple prior methods across several biomedical and general-domain datasets using standard metrics (Spearman’s ρ, cosine, MAP). The mathematical approach appears standard and internally consistent (no derivations/proofs noted as needed), and the experimental methodology uses appropriate baselines and datasets; however, important reproducibility details are omitted in the summary (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and there is no mention of statistical significance testing. Given these minor but relevant omissions and the fact that the method is outperformed on some smaller biomedical datasets, the technical claims are largely but not perfectly supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are generally supported by empirical evidence: experiments across biomedical and general-domain datasets with multiple strong baselines show the proposed weakly-supervised joint embeddings perform competitively, and reported analyses link embeddings to ontology structure. The mathematical approach (adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling and concept-normalized updates) is plausible and no formal derivations appear necessary or incorrect. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned) and no statistical testing is reported; additionally the method is outperformed on some smaller biomedical datasets and phrase-approximation beats direct concept embeddings on real-entity data, which the authors acknowledge. These issues are minor relative to the core technical contribution but prevent a full, flawless endorsement.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper's focus on weakly-supervised joint embedding of concepts, phrases, and words is squarely within representation learning for NLP/ML and is directly relevant to top conferences. It evaluates with standard methods (adapted skip-gram with negative sampling), uses appropriate metrics (Spearman's ρ, MAP) and datasets drawn from both biomedical (UMLS, PubMed, MayoSRS, UMNSRS) and general domains (YAGO/Wikipedia, new real-world entity similarity/relatedness sets), and compares to strong baselines. The inclusion of a novel dataset, thorough related work, and explicit discussion of limitations further align it with the expectations and audience of a top-tier NLP/ML venue. | B(5): Yes — the paper addresses core representation-learning problems (joint embeddings of concepts, phrases, and words) that are squarely within NLP/ML. It proposes a novel weakly-supervised adaptation of skip-gram, evaluates on both biomedical (PubMed, clinical notes, UMLS, MayoSRS/UMNSRS) and general-domain (Wikipedia/YAGO, NYT/Gigaword) datasets, and compares to appropriate baselines using standard metrics (Spearman ρ, cosine, MAP). The methodology, datasets, and evaluation standards are those expected at a top-tier venue, and the work is positioned to interest the NLP/ML community focusing on embeddings and semantic representation learning.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's focus on weakly-supervised joint embedding of concepts, phrases, and words is squarely within representation learning for NLP/ML and is directly relevant to top conferences. It evaluates with standard methods (adapted skip-gram with negative sampling), uses appropriate metrics (Spearman's ρ, MAP) and datasets drawn from both biomedical (UMLS, PubMed, MayoSRS, UMNSRS) and general domains (YAGO/Wikipedia, new real-world entity similarity/relatedness sets), and compares to strong baselines. The inclusion of a novel dataset, thorough related work, and explicit discussion of limitations further align it with the expectations and audience of a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes — the paper addresses core representation-learning problems (joint embeddings of concepts, phrases, and words) that are squarely within NLP/ML. It proposes a novel weakly-supervised adaptation of skip-gram, evaluates on both biomedical (PubMed, clinical notes, UMLS, MayoSRS/UMNSRS) and general-domain (Wikipedia/YAGO, NYT/Gigaword) datasets, and compares to appropriate baselines using standard metrics (Spearman ρ, cosine, MAP). The methodology, datasets, and evaluation standards are those expected at a top-tier venue, and the work is positioned to interest the NLP/ML community focusing on embeddings and semantic representation learning.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words without human corpus annotations—by using ontology-derived representative phrases as distant supervision, and shows embeddings competitive with prior annotation-heavy methods on concept similarity and relatedness. It demonstrates applicability across biomedical (UMLS) and general (YAGO/Wikipedia) domains and contributes a novel real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset, which makes the approach likely to influence practitioners who need scalable concept embeddings. Remaining weaknesses (being outperformed on smaller biomedical sets, not capturing some non-lexical information, and not incorporating ontology structure) limit it from being transformative but still position it as a strong, broadly useful contribution that will open follow-up work incorporating structural ontology signals. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—learning concept, phrase, and word embeddings without any human corpus annotations—by using representative ontology phrases as distant supervision, and shows competitive performance with prior methods that relied on annotated data. It demonstrates applicability across domains (biomedical UMLS and real-world YAGO/Wikipedia) and even provides a new real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset, which could catalyze follow-on work. While not transformative—limitations include being outperformed on smaller biomedical sets, failure to capture some non-lexical ontology information, and phrase-approximations sometimes outperforming direct concept embeddings—the approach meaningfully opens research directions (e.g., integrating ontology structure) and is likely to be adopted for scalable embedding learning.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—learning joint embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words without human corpus annotations—by using ontology-derived representative phrases as distant supervision, and shows embeddings competitive with prior annotation-heavy methods on concept similarity and relatedness. It demonstrates applicability across biomedical (UMLS) and general (YAGO/Wikipedia) domains and contributes a novel real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset, which makes the approach likely to influence practitioners who need scalable concept embeddings. Remaining weaknesses (being outperformed on smaller biomedical sets, not capturing some non-lexical information, and not incorporating ontology structure) limit it from being transformative but still position it as a strong, broadly useful contribution that will open follow-up work incorporating structural ontology signals.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—learning concept, phrase, and word embeddings without any human corpus annotations—by using representative ontology phrases as distant supervision, and shows competitive performance with prior methods that relied on annotated data. It demonstrates applicability across domains (biomedical UMLS and real-world YAGO/Wikipedia) and even provides a new real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset, which could catalyze follow-on work. While not transformative—limitations include being outperformed on smaller biomedical sets, failure to capture some non-lexical ontology information, and phrase-approximations sometimes outperforming direct concept embeddings—the approach meaningfully opens research directions (e.g., integrating ontology structure) and is likely to be adopted for scalable embedding learning.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable new perspective by using ontology representative phrases as distant (weak) supervision to jointly learn concept, phrase, and word embeddings without any human corpus annotations, which distinguishes it from prior supervised or annotation-dependent approaches. Methodologically it adapts skip-gram with negative sampling to a joint EW/EP/EC setup and introduces normalization for multi-concept phrases—an instructive, non-trivial extension rather than a trivial reapplication. The authors also contribute a new real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset, further supporting novelty. However, the core training mechanism is an adaptation of existing embedding techniques and some prior methods (e.g., CHOI) still outperform it on smaller datasets, so the work is notable but not radical. | B(4): The paper proposes a notable novelty by using representative phrases from ontologies as distant (weak) supervision to jointly learn concept, phrase, and word embeddings without any human corpus annotations — a clear methodological departure from prior concept-embedding work (e.g., De Vine, Choi, Mencia, Chiu). It also contributes a new real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset and extensive empirical comparisons that differentiate its approach. That said, the core technique is an adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling (an incremental but non-trivial extension), so the advance is meaningful but not a radical rethinking of representation learning.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a notable new perspective by using ontology representative phrases as distant (weak) supervision to jointly learn concept, phrase, and word embeddings without any human corpus annotations, which distinguishes it from prior supervised or annotation-dependent approaches. Methodologically it adapts skip-gram with negative sampling to a joint EW/EP/EC setup and introduces normalization for multi-concept phrases—an instructive, non-trivial extension rather than a trivial reapplication. The authors also contribute a new real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset, further supporting novelty. However, the core training mechanism is an adaptation of existing embedding techniques and some prior methods (e.g., CHOI) still outperform it on smaller datasets, so the work is notable but not radical.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a notable novelty by using representative phrases from ontologies as distant (weak) supervision to jointly learn concept, phrase, and word embeddings without any human corpus annotations — a clear methodological departure from prior concept-embedding work (e.g., De Vine, Choi, Mencia, Chiu). It also contributes a new real-world entity similarity/relatedness dataset and extensive empirical comparisons that differentiate its approach. That said, the core technique is an adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling (an incremental but non-trivial extension), so the advance is meaningful but not a radical rethinking of representation learning.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experimental evaluation is reasonably broad—multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora (PUBMED, CLINICAL, PM+CL, WIKINYT) and several task datasets (MayoSRS, Mini Mayo SRS, UMNSRS, and new real-world entity similarity/relatedness sets) with many competitive baselines and standard metrics (Spearman, cosine, MAP)—so claims are supported empirically. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters are not provided, and code availability is not mentioned, which hampers reproducibility and understanding of robustness. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses failure cases and limitations (outperforming issues on small biomedical sets, phrase-based approximations beating direct embeddings on real-world entities, missing non-lexical information). | B(3): The experimental evaluation is reasonably broad — multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora, new real-world entity datasets, and comparisons to many prior baselines using Spearman and MAP — and related work coverage is reported as thorough. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are mentioned and hyperparameters are not reported (code availability is also not mentioned), which limits assessment of robustness and reproducibility. The paper does discuss failure cases and limitations (e.g., missing non-lexical information, cases where baselines outperform on small biomedical sets), but the lack of experimental detail and ablations means important analyses are missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is reasonably broad—multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora (PUBMED, CLINICAL, PM+CL, WIKINYT) and several task datasets (MayoSRS, Mini Mayo SRS, UMNSRS, and new real-world entity similarity/relatedness sets) with many competitive baselines and standard metrics (Spearman, cosine, MAP)—so claims are supported empirically. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters are not provided, and code availability is not mentioned, which hampers reproducibility and understanding of robustness. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses failure cases and limitations (outperforming issues on small biomedical sets, phrase-based approximations beating direct embeddings on real-world entities, missing non-lexical information).

reviewer_b.justification: The experimental evaluation is reasonably broad — multiple biomedical and general-domain corpora, new real-world entity datasets, and comparisons to many prior baselines using Spearman and MAP — and related work coverage is reported as thorough. However, no ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are mentioned and hyperparameters are not reported (code availability is also not mentioned), which limits assessment of robustness and reproducibility. The paper does discuss failure cases and limitations (e.g., missing non-lexical information, cases where baselines outperform on small biomedical sets), but the lack of experimental detail and ablations means important analyses are missing.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad and relevant set of baselines (APPROXPHRco, APPROXWORD, PHRASEco, WORD variants, DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU-2/30) and evaluates on standard datasets (PUBMED, CLINICAL, WIKINYT, MayoSRS, UMNSRS, and new real-world entity sets) using common metrics (Spearman's ρ, cosine, MAP). It also acknowledges when prior methods (e.g., CHOI) outperform it on some biomedical datasets and provides analyses of differences and how ontology information is reflected in the embeddings, with thorough related-work coverage. A minor concern for fairness/replicability is that hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which could affect exact comparability. | B(5): The paper compares against a broad and recent set of baselines (e.g., De Vine 2014, Choi 2016, Mencia 2016, Chiu 2016, and multiple word/phrase baselines) and evaluates on the same standard datasets (PUBMED, CLINICAL, WIKINYT, MayoSRS, UMNSRS, and new real-entity sets) using standard metrics (Spearman's ρ, cosine, MAP). The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods outperform theirs (CHOI on smaller biomedical sets; phrase-based approximations on real-entity sets) and discuss what aspects of ontology structure their method captures or misses. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes differences in behavior between their method and baselines, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad and relevant set of baselines (APPROXPHRco, APPROXWORD, PHRASEco, WORD variants, DEVINE, CHOI, MENCIA, CHIU-2/30) and evaluates on standard datasets (PUBMED, CLINICAL, WIKINYT, MayoSRS, UMNSRS, and new real-world entity sets) using common metrics (Spearman's ρ, cosine, MAP). It also acknowledges when prior methods (e.g., CHOI) outperform it on some biomedical datasets and provides analyses of differences and how ontology information is reflected in the embeddings, with thorough related-work coverage. A minor concern for fairness/replicability is that hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which could affect exact comparability.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad and recent set of baselines (e.g., De Vine 2014, Choi 2016, Mencia 2016, Chiu 2016, and multiple word/phrase baselines) and evaluates on the same standard datasets (PUBMED, CLINICAL, WIKINYT, MayoSRS, UMNSRS, and new real-entity sets) using standard metrics (Spearman's ρ, cosine, MAP). The authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods outperform theirs (CHOI on smaller biomedical sets; phrase-based approximations on real-entity sets) and discuss what aspects of ontology structure their method captures or misses. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper analyzes differences in behavior between their method and baselines, supporting a comprehensive and fair comparison.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow: it presents the adapted skip-gram method (including EW, EP, EC notation), then experiments across biomedical and Wikipedia datasets, and concludes with limitations. The main contributions are stated early and related work and baselines are covered thoroughly, and the notation for concept/phrase/word embeddings is explicit. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not make clear whether figures/tables fully support all claims. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow and clearly states its main contributions early (the summary lists clear main claims and a focused methodology adapting skip-gram to jointly learn EW/EP/EC). Notation and method presentation seem coherent from the description (methodology and datasets are specified) and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related-work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility and practical clarity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow: it presents the adapted skip-gram method (including EW, EP, EC notation), then experiments across biomedical and Wikipedia datasets, and concludes with limitations. The main contributions are stated early and related work and baselines are covered thoroughly, and the notation for concept/phrase/word embeddings is explicit. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and the summary does not make clear whether figures/tables fully support all claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a logical flow and clearly states its main contributions early (the summary lists clear main claims and a focused methodology adapting skip-gram to jointly learn EW/EP/EC). Notation and method presentation seem coherent from the description (methodology and datasets are specified) and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related-work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned, which reduces reproducibility and practical clarity.

main_claims: Proposes a weakly-supervised method to jointly learn embeddings for concepts, phrases, and words by using representative phrases from an ontology as distant supervision over unannotated text.

Method requires no human corpus annotations and trains concept embeddings from phrase contexts, producing embeddings competitive with prior methods that used human annotations on concept similarity and relatedness tasks.

Demonstrates embeddings for biomedical concepts (UMLS) and real-world entities (YAGO/Wikipedia), and presents a novel dataset of similarity and relatedness for real-world entities identified by Wikipedia page and text phrase.

Finds that the learned embeddings reflect aspects of ontology structure: concepts and their representative phrases are embedded close together and concepts of the same semantic type tend to cluster, though some non-lexical information is not captured.
_summary.methodology: Adapts the skip-gram with negative sampling model to jointly learn word (EW), phrase (EP), and concept (EC) embedding matrices by treating occurrences of known phrases as distant supervision for their concepts and training on tagged and untagged corpora using sliding context windows; concept updates are normalized by the number of concepts a phrase can represent.
datasets: CLINICAL (Ohio State Wexner Medical Center clinical notes)
WIKINYT (2015 English Wikipedia + NYT portions of Gigaword)
limitations: On the two smaller biomedical datasets the method is outperformed by CHOI.
On the real-world entity datasets, phrase-based concept approximations outperform the direct concept embeddings.
Some non-lexical information about concepts is not being captured by the method; authors suggest including ontology structure directly could help.
Analyses are restricted to evaluations relying on the affine organization of the vector space and optimized hyperparameters may change for downstream tasks.
_summary.topic_area: Representation learning / NLP — joint embeddings of concepts, phrases, and words (biomedical and general domains)
